note: The bard praises King Kitekele as a bull who defends the herd fro other bulls and represents it with an identity proclaimed far and wide. "Appear" is is the greeting given to a king to recognize his presence; he becomes visible socially, as the sun does radiantly, passing from behind a cloud.

passage of linegroups (1-2, 3-4, 5-7, 8) that cohere by parallel syntax (3-4: noun initial (cf 43-44); 5-6: possessive particle initial (cf 503-504)), by common semantic features (6-7: wild animal (cf 504-505)), and by repetition (1-2, 8) that creates a pattern in aba .
Scourge, or switch, akati, literally a small wooden stick, which can be used to discipline a child. This image of praise is elaborated on line 46.

A leopard causes howls of pain among hunting dogs tethered outside, which it attacks at night. The audacious and unpredictable leopard also attacks humans. Kahigi I was the last, strong, Hinda monarch that ruled before Kitekele siezed control of the throne. The two kings were also related by marriage, as Kahigi's wife Matiti was a member of the Bankango clan, as was Kitekele.

A dream also bears important information at the opening of Kachwenyanja . The first scene of Kaitaba, the epic about Kitekele's son, portrays the king's troubled, sleepless night, as does another classic epic ballad, Muhata. Night and dreams frame the ensuing action within the dimensions of an unseen world, with which some actors resonate.

stanza in aabb b formed by contrast between lines that contain an initial subordinate verb and a main indicative verb ( a 's: 9, 10) and lines that begin with "head" and contain a main subjunctive verb.
Tying stones on one's head or applying kaiyula herb are said to be treatments for headache.
These lines were not sung during the performance recorded. I inserted them at the request of the singer.

These fish are symbols of feminine virtues. The one named bifu (by appearance a kind of catfish) and the one alluded to as Ebitabologa (literally, those-which-do-not-bellow - emamba , the lungfish) symbolize smoothness of skin and bravery under duress, respectively. The meaning of the latter trope is explored in the note to The Place You Come From : 20.

passage of three couplets; the first (16-17) and the last (20-21) cohere by semantics: respectively, of fish and of praisenames; the second (18-19) coheres by parallelism; the addition of these lines was requested by the singer after his performance.

These lines of praise seem to have a parallel rhetorical structure: among seats, the bride deserves the throne; among roads that help construct identity (cf., The Place You Come From : 16-18), hers is the major thoroughfare to the kingdom of Bugabo.
Bunyana , literally, Place-of-calfs, accords the bride high prestige. Like the king-bull tropes, it articulates a poetics of cattle which will be very productive in the epic.
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines that represent a 1st person's thoughts and acts ( a 's: 22, 24) and lines that represent those of a "little woman" ( b 's: 23, 25).
Enkuku are the hand's middle knuckles one uses to knock on a door, not the upper ones you might apply to an adversary's jaw.
compound stanza in aba formed by linegroups (each beginning with a locutive) that contrast by conversational turns: the wife's ( a 's:26-28, 31-32) and the husband's ( b : 29-30).

This passage of verbal abuse is structured as a conditional relationship between clauses that are contrary-to-fact and hyperbolic, respectively. This is an instance of the rhetorical figure known as adynaton. The bard uses the same rhetorical structure later at a climactic moment, 250-254
stanza in a a bab formed by semantic contrast between subordinate clauses ( a 's: 33-34, 36) and principal clauses ( b 's: 35, 37).

verse passage in abab ab formed by short, generalized narrative descriptions of the "little women" ( a 's: 38-39, 50, 55) alternating with linegroups that form stanzaic patterns ( b 's: 40-49.1, 51-54, 56-61); the first such linegroup is itself a verse passage formed in aba by contrast between a repeated exchange ( a 's: 40-41, 49-49.1) and lines of praise ( b 's: 42-48); the second such linegroup, completely composed of lines in which the direct object precedes the verb, is a stanza formed in abab by contrast between lines in which a prepositional phrase is final ( a 's: 51, 53) and lines in which the verb is final ( b 's: 52, 54); the third such linegroup is a compound stanza formed in abb by contrast between a single line composed like those immediately preceding, direct object + verb phrase ( a : 56) and linegroups that elaborate this composition by enjambment combined with repetition or apposition ( b 's: 57-59 and 60-61, respectively).

Muzinza (the more common, Swahili-based spelling) or Muzinja (as it is said in Luhaya) refers to King Kitekele. He is called the Muzinza because his clan, the Bankango of Kyamtwara, asserted its origin in the group of kingdoms to the south of Hayaland known as Buzinja or Zinzaland.

45 The symbolic Ant Bear, destroyer of termite mounds, is praise for Kitekele. These mounds are strong images of Hinda rule, doubly meaningful as landmarks of a royally organized insect society and as the form deceased Hinda spirits create when they emerge from the ground.

The image in lines 4 and 44 becomes more explicit. The scourge or little stick is domesticated as a little branch ( akashanju ), bringing into sharper focus the metaphor of a parent disciplining a child.

47 Lyangombe is a ruling spirit from the southern part of Hayaland and beyond. Some place his origin in Congo. He occupies the same exalted position that Wamala does in the Bachwezi pantheon of the north (see Bjerke 1981, 53, for his place among the Bazinza). In a version of the ballad Mugasha from southern Hayaland, the fisher-god battles Lyangombe (instead of Wamara) for his daughter's hand in marriage. Kabale is the royal palace in Kyamutwala, the seat of Kitekele's kingship.

Kitekele is a seed bull as the founder of the Bankango dynasty. Virility is a principal part of Kitekere's royal symbolism, also seen in the attention paid to his royal mistresses (lines 143-144 and 317-319).
Variants of this phrase are used by the singer to punctuate the narrative eight times in Kitekele and nine times in Kaitaba .
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines composed as verb + [hide] of + animal ( a 's: 63, 64 - word order reversed between them) and a single line that begins with a direct object.
Point-down is the position of a spear held in readiness while travelling in a dangerous place.

compound stanza in a x ab , 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast between couplets that cohere by parallelism ( a 's:66-67, [68 is interpolated], 69-70) and a single couplet that coheres by enjambment ( b : 71-72).
The ferryman Bijuli is evidently well known personage, for he also marks a journey through Ntoija and Bukongo in the epic ballad of King Kahigi sung by Muzee.

compound stanza in aa a b , 2 + 2 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets ( a 's: 73-74, 75-76, 77-78) comprised of the quoted speech of a single speaker - which also cohere by enjambment (73-74, 75-76) or repetition (77-78) - contrasted with a single couplet ( b : 79-80) comprised of the quoted speech of two speakers - which also coheres by repetition.

These sighs are voiced. They are named by the same verb ( oku-sinda ) that describes the roar of a lion or a bull. The meaningful connection between such deep-voiced sighing and material needs is also evoked in Kajango : 5. As a whole, the line means 'pay me.'
stanza in abab formed by contrast between lines that begin with ninye + prepositional phrase ( a 's: 88, 90) and lines that are not so composed ( b 's: 89, 91.

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, of couplets composed as 0verb + placename/ verb phrase + placename, formed by contrast between couplets whose second line has an initial verb ( a 's: 92-93, 96-96) and a single couplet whose second line begins with a direct object ( b : 94-95).
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines comprised of prepositional phrases ( a 's: 99, 100) and a single line comprised of a main clause ( b : 101).

verse of two compound stanzas, 2 + 1 + 2 and 2 + 3 + 2, which have essentially the same final couplets; they are both comprised of quoted speech and formed in aba by contrast in speakers: the Abayondo guards ( a 's: 102-103, 105-106 and 107-108, 112-113) and the spirit medium ( b 's: 104 and 109-111).
This line was not sung but inserted by me at the singer's request.

This court officer controlled access to the king's court. His name, Gahulwa, derives from okw-ahula , "to pick over and select," as one removes small stones in preparing rice or separates truth from falsehood in listening to the news. Gahulwa chose who and what would be passed to the king.
compound stanza in aab , 2 + 2 + 2, formed by contrast in speakers: the Abayondo guards ( a 's: 114-115, 116-117) and the spirit medium ( b : 118-119).
stanza in aa a b formed by contrast between lines that name gifts ( a 's: 120, 121, 122) and a single line composed only of verbs ( b : 123).

A king was called the Straightener: he restored equlibrium through just judicial decisions; he dispensed rewards for service to the state; he punished wrongdoing; and through ritual and other means he maintained relationships with unseen spiritual forces.

stanza in ab b ab formed by lines that contain golola "sets (things) straight" in a query or an assertion of the guards' ignorance ( a 's: 129, 132) contrasted with solutions to the puzzle, first ironic, then direct ( b 's:130, 131, 133).
stanza in abab by contrast between lines composed as "when he gives you" + named asset ( a 's: 134, 136) and lines that praise or qualify that act ( b 's: 135, 137).
The word translated as "family" is eka . More properly a "household," an eka may vary in the types of kin included within it.

These fragmentary glimpses of court life (like those in Kajango : 389-389.2) portray the dangers of its powerful hierarchy, here, accusations and submissive pleas for forgiveness made by clutching the king's knees.

a compound stanza, 4 + 4 + 6, formed in aab by two pairs of couplets ( a 's: 139-142, 143-146), each pair composed of one couplet that depicts the king's authority or virility (139-140, 143-144 whose parallel lines are composed respectively as troop name + verb phrase, and verb phrase + woman's name) and one couplet that depicts a mankala game (141-142, 145-146, whose parallel lines are composed respectively as ..."he plays" + strategy name, and man's name + verb phrase) contrasted with a linegroup ( b : 147-152) of three couplets that in turn form a figure of aba by contrast between two couplets composed as personal names/ appositive + verb phrase ( a 's: 147-148, 151-152) and a single couplet that coheres by repetition ( b : 149-150).

Mankala in Hayaland is called olwesho and is played on a board of four rows of eight cups with 64 pea-size counters. Similar equipment is used in other east African societies - Rwanda, Ganda, Swahili, et al - but is played with different rules. Haya olwesho has conventional openings, and contestants choose strategically from among them. There are many such opening arrangements of counters in cups, among them those named in lines 141-142, 'stockpile' and 'cattle herd.' Play progresses as contestants choose among possible moves. Typical strategic sequences in play are figuratively named with verbal phrases like those mentioned in lines 145-146, 'bled cattle with a dart' and 'tied the banana bast.' Strategic play requires mentally keeping track of complexly embedded strings of numbers, a capability not unlike that evident in the more elaborate stanzaic structures of heroic verse.
Shield-breaker is the praisename for a resident of Bugabo kingdom, whose first Hinda king, Nkwele, was a contemporary of Kitekele, according to the sources consulted by Cory (1959).

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, formed in abb by two couplets composed as personal names/ verb phrase comprising distant past + present continuitive ( b 's: 156-157, 158-159) contrasted with a single couplet ( a : 154-155) whose lines cohere by a inversion of syntax and semantics: initial verb (descend) + prepositional phrase/ prepositional phrase + final verb (climb).
stanza formed by inversion of composition in succeeding couplets: dependent phrase (prepositional)/ main clause (162-163); main clause/ dependent phrase (praise).
Guardhouse translates bulwani, literally, "place of the Balwani clan," the clan entrusted with the duty of guarding the palace in the kingdom of Kyamutwala

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2, formed in aab by contrast in speakers: spirit medium ( a 's: 166-167, 168-169) and palace guard ( b : 170-171); each couplet coheres internally by parallelism and repetition
stanza of two triplets, a variant of aabb , formed by three parallel lines ( a 's: 172-174) contrasted with a triplet ( b 's: 175-177) that forms a pattern of aab by repetition and contrast.

two three-line linegroups associated by repetiton of the initial word in their final lines, "I saw" (180, 183); lines in the first linegroup (178-180) do not seem to cohere, but those in the second linegroup (181-183) form a stanza in aab by contrast between prepositional phrases ( a 's: 181, 182) and a principal clause ( b : 183).

With this ornate salutation to the king in his palace at Kabale, the key episode of the heroic ballad begins, its Call to action. This is a very elaborate one. The greeting begins like its cognate in Kachwenyanja (lines 69-69.2) but greatly amplifies the theme of power difference between the spirit-medium and the founder of the Bankango dynasty.

compound stanza in aa aa b , 3 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 1 formed by contrast between linegroups that cohere internally by repetition and/or parallelism ( a 's: 184-186, 187-188, 189-190, 191-193) and a single, unique line.
stanza in aabb b formed by contrast between lines composed as dependent verb + main clause ( a 's: 195, 196) and lines composed as "my lord" + "head" + verb phrase ( b 's: 197, 198, 199).

The name Munyaitoju means "resident of Itoju village." According to the historical traditions recorded by Kaijage, Munyaitoju was Kitekele's distant cousin. Munyaitoju's father, Kitekele's father's sister's husband, was the last Hinda king of Kyamutwala, Kalemera II, so, in this version of Kymutwalan history, Munyitoju was a rival for the throne that Kitekele and his father, Rushuma, wrested from Hinda control.

compound stanza, 3 + 3 + 2, formed in aab by contrast between three-line linegroups that name the cattle raider ( a 's: 200-201, 202-205 - repeated proper name and serial praisenames, respectively) and a couplet that describes his actions ( b : 206-207) and coheres by parallel use of final prepositional phrases.
Climbs a buffalo's face is a praise that signifies fearlessness.

Munyaitoju's burning of a royal drum is a story unknown to me. Burning, is an act of war and proper to warrior praise, but the drum could be a kingdom, or a kingship, or just the royal instrument and ensign itself.
stanza in abcb formed by contrast between lines that cohere by parallel use of a relative clause ( b 's: 209, 210.1) and those that do not ( a and c : 208, 210, respectively).
Here called simply enjoka (snake), an individual's life serpent is said to be the center of personhood, the seat of thinking and desire, as in Rukiza 171.2.

stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that describe a culturally expected sequence ( a 's: 211, 212, 213) and one that breaks that sequence with an unexpected intrusion ( b : 214 - like the ripening millet sequence in Kaitaba).

The cry heard is a ululation, like the keening a woman does when she learns of a death in the family. Or it may be the sound of an alarm raised to announce the advent of another kind unlucky event. Or it may just be the noise of high spirits raised in celebration. This extended puzzle of interpretation, its eventual solution, together with the spirit-medium's report of his dream, forms the richly meaningful episode that generates the ethical Call to action.

stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that repeat "what" + "alarm" ( a 's: 215, 216 - reversed word order in the latter) and a single line that repeats "alarm" but embeds it in a different sentence ( b : 217).

Big-cheeks, Hunter's roar, Rustling (like the sound of new fabric), Pisser-in-the-marsh, Worn-by-the-beautiful (referring to the hide), and others are praisenames for the animals whose pursuit in hunting is proposed as the possible origin of the puzzling cry. These epithets would be shouted when such animals are killed. Praise of the slain animal amplifies the hunter's identity, as in Kajango : 80.1-83.1. The Apportioner-of-trails (234) confuses hunting dogs by continually backtracking, giving each dog a different trail to follow. The omunyima , a hyrax, is said to low like a mother cow - a praise that notes the difference created by motherhood (see also Kajango : 332). The bard elaborates the episode by quoting courtiers' attempts to interpret the unsettling cries. Hunters' chants (and their absence) become evidence to evaluate alternate hypotheses.

two stanzas based on an abb pattern formed by contrast between lines composed as "perhaps they killed" + animal name ( a 's) and lines composed as "I would have heard" + animal praisename ( b 's); the first stanza (218-221) is formed in x abb by the use of an introductory line (218) followed by a line composed as a (219), followed by two b 's (220, 221); in the second stanza (222-227), the abb pattern is formed by one line composed as a (222), followed by a line composed as b (223), followed by an expansion of the expected, final b into a four line stanza in abab formed by contrst between lines composed as "I would have heard" + praisename ( a 's: 224, 226) and slightly different lines that are substantial repetitions of each other ( b 's: 225, 227).

verse passage of four linegroups (228-231, 232-235, 236-238, 239-241) formed in aabb by contrast between the linegroups composed as "alarm" / "they might have killed" + animal/ ["I would have heard" +] animal praisename ( a 's:228-231, 232-235; in the former, the first line is expanded to two lines; in the latter, the last line is so expanded) and the linegroups composed similarly but lacking the first compositional unit, "alarm"/, and expanding the final line to two ( b 's: 236-238, 239-241).

compound stanza in aab formed by two five-line linegroups ( a 's: 244-248 and 249-253) contrasted with a final line of great thematic significance ( b : 254); both initial linegroups are composed internally as abb by contrast between single lines composed as "when" + past perfect verb... ( a 's: 244 and 249) and couplets that cohere internally by repetition and parallelism ( b 's: 245-246, 247-248 and 250-251, 252-253).

This same rhetorical figure, a species of adynaton, was used by the spirit medium to castigate his wife (33-37). Imaginary, hyperbolic violence proclaim the virtues that impel the speaker. A passion for knowledge of the unseen world drives the spirit medium to envision violence to his beloved new bride. The cowherd's love of cattle impels him to speak of violence even to so strong and dangerous a king as Kitekele. News of the unseen and love of cattle (the first subordinate to the second) motivate and frame the two principal narrative sequences.
Kabushumba and Kaitaba are Kitekele's sons (see Kaitaba : 376) as, it seems, is Kagambi.
Gentle-feet is a praisename for calfs, while sorgham tassels evoke the tufted tails of cattle (257).

verse of two stanzas in aab formed by contrast between multiple lines that refer to the theft of cattle in general or in specific ( a 's: 255-256 and 258-260) and single lines that contain a synedochic description of the disappearing cattle ( b 's: 257 and 261)

Details about the king's beer and its container mark the importance of beer in the royal court (see also Kajango :358-370, Kachwenyanja :66-71, Kaitaba :80-89). Here the king's use of beer signals his transition from peaceful repose to warlike action.

compound stanza, 3 + 3 + 2 , formed in aab by contrast between triplets whose lines cohere internally by stanzaic patterning ( a 's: 262-264, 265-266.1) and a couplet whose lines cohere by parallel use of a final prepositional phrase ( b : 267-268); triplets are formed respectively as aab and aba by contrast between multiple lines that cohere by repetition and/or parallelism ( a 's:262, 263 and 265,267) and single lines that both contain "What shall I do?" ( b 's: 264 and 266).

1 Milk vessels are powerful symbols of household wealth and status, carefully carved, decorated and cared for. They honor a family head who has the social standing to possess cattle - here the king himself, the ultimate owner of all cattle. In their customary position at the hearth, they embody domesticity and well-being. Milk vessels are also images of brides, sent with them on their journies to their new families, wrapped with pure-white papyrus strands and attached to their permanent storage poles (see Kaiyula : 270-271.

compound stanza - of couplets composed as placename/ verb phrase - formed in aba by linegroups of couplets whose verb phrase contains "proclaims itself in flames" ( a 's: 269-274 and 279-282) contrasted with a linegroup whose verb phrase does not indicate destruction ( b : 275-278).

stanza in ab b a' formed by contrast between lines of quoted speech that begin, "He said, 'What shall I do...'" ( a 's: 283, 286) and lines of narrative that are composed solely of verbs ( b 's: 284, 285).
Striking poses, enacting gestures that embody royal virtues of strength and virility seems to have been a notable part of the discourse of the royal court (see Kaitaba : 252-255).

compound stanza of couplets composed as dependent verb + body part/ 2nd person locutive + sentence, in which the second line describes a visual metaphor for the image contained in the first; the compound stanza is formed in aa a b by contrast between couplets whose second line begins with a locutive ( a 's: 287-288, 289-290, 291-292) and a couplet that lacks the locutive and appends "king" the the end of the preceding line ( b : 293-294).

These doctors, or bafumu , are specialists in augury, in reading signs of events that are occurring elsewhere or that have not yet occurred. They are also specialists in the offensive and defensive use of magical charms. When possible, the names of bafumu are translated because they seem to have more clear and unusual meanings than other personal names.
stanza in abb formed by contrast between narrative lines composed as "they called" + personal name ( b 's: 296-297) and a single line of quoted speech ( a : 295).
Bwelanyange is the site of a famous spirit shrine.
stanza in abb formed by contrast between lines composed as ...3rd person verb + direct object ( b 's: 305, 306) and a single line composed of three verbs ( a : 304).
stanza in abb formed by contrast between lines that contain "sound" + "drums" ( b 's: 307.1-308, order reversed in the second) and a single formulaic line ( a : 307).
This line was inserted at the request of the singer.
Those addressed on these lines are apparantly servants. Their names have the following meanings: Telikunda (309) "Never gives in," Byabato (309) "Children's matters" (311)

compound stanza, 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 3, formed in aa aa b by couplets composed as personal name(s)/..."you bind up provisions" ( a 's: 309-310, 311-312, 313-314, 315-316) contrasted with three lines ( b : 317-319) that begin similarly, but vary the established pattern.

This allusive, riddle-like name used in address, May-it-not-find-me, together with its answer, The-eye-of-a-hateful-one, marks the addressee as a muzana , a female slave. This call-and-response sequence is like that which names the muzana in Kachwenyanja: 188-188.1. The order to bind charms indicates that she is one of the King's mistresses.
stanza in aabb b formed by contrast between lines composed as "he summoned" + personal name ( a 's: 320-321) and lines that begin with a locutive ( b 's:322-324).

This self praise ( ebyebugo ) was recited extemporaneously by a member of the audience. The bard continues to play the enanga, but the reciter chants seemingly without regard to its melody. The recitation occurs within the frame of the depicted events: with this genre warriors encouraged and identified themselves in preparation for battle. The imagery of snakes, healing, and spirits is probably connected to reciter's calling as a mufumu (see above, line 295). But this is not the reciter's personal self-praise per se; it is rather a performance done to support and augment the ballad of Kitekele .
What climbs a tree with no fingers or claws is a snake (below, 339-342).
Crow also appears as a principal character in the spirit world in Kajango , line 354.
Kashasila, "She-who-feels-another's-pain," is the spirit connected with the reciter's clan, Balwani of Kyamtwala. She is one of the spirits who assisted him in his practice as a mufumu .
At Buzongola, "Place of travelers," grew a tree sacred to Wamala, king of the Bachwezi, according to a version of the epic Muhata - another association between the speaker and the spirit world.
The king might be addressed as Father-creator for his decisive role in creating material well being (see Kaitaba : 4-5).
These spears refers figuratively to warriors gathered for battle (see also Kachwenyanja 54ff) and literally to the actual spears brandished when warriors would recite their self praises.

compound stanza in abb , 1 + 2 + 2, formed by couplets composed as subjunctive verb + placename/ subjunctive verb(s) + "house lane of the Bakulwa" ( b 's: 351.1-352, 354-355) contrasted with a single line marked by aside, adverbial past perfect verb, and repetition ( a : 351).
The Bakulwa clan in Kyamutwala were the preparers of the chief's coffee (Cory and Hartnoll). On the social and ritual uses of coffee, see Kajango : 54n, 433n, and 435n.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that comprise dependent clauses ( a 's:356-357) and a single line that comprises the main clause ( b : 358).

Monkey is the primary totem of the ruling Hinda clan. Claiming to be a (maternal) grandchild justifies Kitekele's joking and refers to his marriage to a Hinda woman. A man customarily joked with his grandparents and preferably married a woman of his grandmother's clan.

compound stanza in aa a b , 2 + 2 + 2 + 1, formed by contrast between couplets that cohere by repetition and/or semantic association ( a 's: 359-360, 361-362, 363-364) and a single line that completes the logic of the passage.

This self praise is recited by the same member of the audience who recited before. In it, he assumes the persona of Ngimbwa (literally, "He who is compelled"). A close friend of the singer, the reciter apparantly takes the character from another of Abdullah Feza's pieces, Kaitaba . Thus do Kitelele : 367-368 resemble Kaitaba 131-132 and Kitekele : 374-375.1 resemble Kaitaba 132-134.
, 382: The unclean tasks and unspeakable words are acts of sorcery performed on the king's behalf by royal sorcerers ( bafumu ).

The troops of warriors named and praised were recruited on a geographical basis. The Self-Invited is a euphemism for death - in most people's experience, he is not summoned, but comes anyway. Shield-breakers is the praisename for residents of Bugabo kingdom, whose King was Nkwele (393).
stanza in aa a b contrast between lines composed as "go with" + battalion name ( a 's: 283, 284, 285) and a single line composed as double verb + prepositional phrase ( b : 386).

stanza in aba a b bbbb formed by contrast between lines composed as "go with" + battalion name ( a 's: 388, 390, 391) and lines comprised of praisenames ( b 's: 389, 391.1, 392, 393, 394, 395). intro line

Fluid movement in Nkwele's praise (393) refers to the Hinda origin of this first king of Bugabo. A mulamula tree was used to mark boundaries (Cory 116) and has a mythical charter for doing so (Schmidt 116).
A Muhamba is a native of Kihanja kingdom
Babwiko is said to be a term of address in Ihangiro regional speech

compound stanza in aba formed by contrast between couplets whose lines cohere by their initial exclamation ( a 's: 399-400, 401-402) and a couplet whose lines cohere by a repeated verb stem ( b : 401-402).
Involundary urination occurs as a result of being struck by an arrow.
This utterance about tonjotonjo wood seems to express a mistaken belief in invulnerability, like Macbeth's confidence inspired by the prophesy about Birnham wood.
stanza in aab formed by contrast between lines that share a verb stem ( a 's: 407-408) and a single line that repeats part of the final line of the preceding figure ( b : 409)
, 415: He said refers to Kitekele.

Slaughter Mountain was the site where King Kalemera, the fourth Hinda king of Kyamutwala, created a stream from the summit to the base of the mountain with the blood of humans and cattle (Kaindoa in Schmidt 299, Cory 53-54)
Mr. Kataishi, one of those gathered to listen to the performance, was introduced as a member of the royal clan (Bankango) and closely related to the line of kings.
A Muyoza is a native of Kyamutwala kingdom.
This self praise was performed by the singer himself
These lines resemble the description of a character in Kaitaba : 130-135.
Butono, here a proper name, as a common noun refers to an infant from the age of three months to the time it begins to walk.

In traditional belief, a latent fetus is one that has been conceived by intercourse but lies dormant for months without developing. This is consistent with the traditional ritual practice of bisisi , through which a dead man could be said to have fathered a child with his widow more than nine months after his death, thereby gaining a male heir and avoiding the loss of property from the father's line.

stanza in aba a b ab formed by contrast betwen lines composed as preposition + placename (462, 464, 465 (+ initial verb), 467) and lines composed as "...they cut and the earth reddened" ( b 's:463, 466, 468).
The men of Buganguzi were infamous warriors ( Kachwenyanja : 184.1).
Banyaihangilo are denizens of Ihangilo kingdom.
The victorious denigrate the vanquished by referring to them with a diminutive, as the spirit medium did with his wives (22).
Ntale means Lion.
Such-and-such ( nanka ) refers to the King's penis. The virility of the elder man is an important part of his identity.
The reciter of this self praise also recited the passages on lines 326-350 and 366-378.

These images refer to the chicken - immunity from lightning and leg spurs - and are an allusion to the clan of the reciter's mother. The Basaizi of Kyamutwala had a chicken as a clan brother ( mulumuna ), or secondary totem.
